title: Mateo Jimenez-Sotelo
text:
UNAM Email
  
  
    
     GitHub
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
      ORCID
  
  
      Bluesky
  

  
  
I am a third-year undergraduate student in Genomic Sciences at the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), based at the Center for Genomic Sciences (CCG).
My research lies at the intersection of computational biology, systems biology, bioinformatics, transcriptomics, and evolutionary genomics. I am interested in developing computational methods to understand biological systems, from gene regulatory networks to ancient human populations.
Currently, I contribute to research in two areas:

Systems Biology — investigating causal discovery methods for reconstructing gene regulatory networks from observational transcriptomic data, while exploring transformer-based language models for biomedical NLP and transcriptomic metadata harmonization.
Population & Evolutionary Genomics — studying population structure in colonial-era individuals from New Spain using principal component analysis (PCA) and admixture-based approaches.

Beyond research, I enjoy developing reproducible bioinformatics workflows, scientific software, and computational tools using Python, R, Bash, and the Linux ecosystem.


Undergraduate Researcher

Systems Biology Laboratory — Center for Genomic Sciences (CCG), UNAM





Population & Evolutionary Genomics Laboratory — International Laboratory for Human Genome Research (LIIGH), UNAM





B.Sc. in Genomic Sciences, UNAM (2024–present)

title: Research experience
section: International Laboratory for Human Genome Research, UNAM
text:
International Laboratory for Human Genome Research, UNAM



Juriquilla, Mexico | Jan 2025 – May 2026


 Undergraduate researcher
PI: Maria Avila-Arcos, Population & Evolutionary Genomics Lab.

Performed principal component analysis (PCA) and ADMIXTURE analyses of ancient and present-day genomic datasets.
Integrated ancient individuals from La Conchita and Hospital San José de los Naturales with reference panels from the 1000 Genomes Project and the Mexican Biobank (MXB).
Processed genotype and sequencing datasets using PLINK, SAMtools, BCFtools, EIGENSOFT (smartpca), ADMIXTOOLS, and Pong.
Generated publication-quality visualizations and contributed to the interpretation of ancestry patterns in colonial-era individuals from New Spain.
Contributed to the population genomics component of the Ancient Pathogens of New Spain project.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?

title: Beyond Science
text:
I’m an enthusiastic Linux user and a strong supporter of open-source software. I love exploring different distros, experimenting with desktop environments and window managers, and learning more about the Unix ecosystem. Lately, I’ve been using Arch Linux and learning Hyprland, while refining my command-line workflow and customizing my desktop through dotfiles, terminal applications, and window manager configurations.
In my opinion, Linux is not only an operating system, but a platform for learning and experimentation!
Cycling is my favorite way to disconnect from the computer and explore new places, particularly when my routes take me along open roads, winding gravel trails, or bustling city streets. Whether it’s a short ride or an all-day adventure, cycling has become an important part of my daily life!
Music has always been part of my life. I’m mainly drawn to jazz, classical, progressive rock, and rock en español. I studied double bass and even performed in an orchestra, an experience that taught me discipline, teamwork, and careful listening. Over the years, I’ve learned to play several instruments, including flute, bass, and double bass.
